comments.text: A historical comment. Both Erdos and Lothar Collatz were participants in the ICM 1950 in Harvard. Erdos was invited speaker, Collatz normal participant without official talk (but in the breaks he spread "his" 3n+1 problem like a salesman). There is a big photo online showing the whole conference group: https://legacy-www.math.harvard.edu/history/icm1950/photos/conference2-l.jpg On the left boarder Lothar Collatz directly right of his former boss Alwin Walther (from the wartime days at TH Darmstadt) can be seen. Here enlarged: https://althofer.de/icm-1950-walther-collatz-2.jpg Collatz is the egghead in the orange frame. Journalist Annette Erbe had identified Collatz (in 2023) in that photo. Who can identify Paul Erdos in the big (linked) photo? Kind regards, Ingo.

In anticipation of a large number of proposed comments to this problem, and in my capacity as a moderator for this site, let me quote two sections from the general comment policy: "Low-value comments that I judge to be written by AI may be not shown and/or deleted at my discretion." "Long detailed formal proofs of problems should not be posted as comments; if you have such a proof (and have checked yourself, as a human, that it is correct), you are encouraged to write it up as a preprint in the traditional fashion and submit it to a journal. There is no firm rule about what is 'too long/detailed', and this is heavily context dependent." I will also take this opportunity to link to two of my pieces of career advice: "Don't prematurely obsess on a single "big problem" or "big theory"" and "Don't base career decisions on glamour or fame".

Yes, thanks for reminding everyone. I would also like to request that people not post general references/related results etc about Collatz here, since there are many other sources for this information. This problem does not need any additional publicity from this site. I added it mainly to record the history about the link between Erdős and this problem. As such, please try to limit your comments here to the intersection of Erdős and Collatz.
